figures.caption: Figure 3: Overview of BARD-VL. (a) Pipeline. We use the packed training layout [𝑄, 𝑥1, 𝑥𝑡 ], where the multimodal context 𝑄 , the clean response 𝑥1, and the corrupted response 𝑥𝑡 are processed in a single sequence. The two objective boxes correspond to the standard masked diffusion loss Lmask and the mixed-noise loss Lmix. (b) Attention mask. Compared with the vanilla block-wise mask, our packed layout removes the redundant duplication of 𝑄 . The 𝑖-th noisy block 𝑥𝑖𝑡 attends only to the shared context 𝑄 , the causal prefix {𝑥01 , . . . , 𝑥𝑖−11 }, and tokens inside the same noisy block. (c) Mixed-noise scheduler. The coefficients 𝜅1𝑡 , 𝜅 2 𝑡 , 𝜅 3 𝑡 control the interpolation among clean tokens, uniformly corrupted visible tokens, and masked tokens.

Figure 2: The autoregressive VLM produces next-token logits under clean causal prefix states, while the dVLM predicts same-position logits under corrupted diffusion states. This mismatch makes direct AR-to-dVLM KL supervision poorly aligned.
Table 4: Ablation of cross-stage dVLM distillation on BARD-VL4B at target block sizes 𝐵 ∈ {8, 16, 32}. The diffusion teacher is the fixed small-block anchor checkpoint 𝜃 (1) .
Table 2: Ablation study on block sizes and initialization strategies.
Table 1: Comparison on seven multimodal benchmarks spanning reasoning, general VQA, and document understanding. For BARD-VL, we report the final large-block checkpoint evaluated at 𝐵 = 4 and 32.
